content: What is the best data structure for a map with addresses as keys? There is xarray, genradix, rbtree, hashtable, rhashtable. Thoughts?
There is also radix_tree, although that is deprecated in favor of xarray
I think xarray is the way. It is used for that purpose in the page cache I think.

But it says this?

The XArray implementation is efficient when the indices used are densely clustered; hashing the object and using the hash as the index will not perform well.
Afaik kprobes need to map addresses to structs in the breakpoint handler, maybe worth seeing how they do it.

Alice Ryhl said:

But it says this?

The XArray implementation is efficient when the indices used are densely clustered; hashing the object and using the hash as the index will not perform well.

I did not dig into xarray, but I think it is similar to radix tree. So a bad input could lead to really bad performance. But in general pointers (shifted down to be page indexes) would be densly clustered? If your input is uniformly random with very low key space utilization, maybe it is not a good structure.

It would not take long to benchmark it against the other structures.
Shifted down to page indexes? I could have two pointers in the same page.

Alice Ryhl said:

Shifted down to page indexes? I could have two pointers in the same page.

I understand, I am trying to illustrate the intended use for xarray as I understand it. It is designed to map addresses to backing store in the page cache, I think. These would be densely clustered. If your keys have a different distribution because they have a different nature of origin, maybe it will not be efficient. But again, you could benchmark it relatively fast.
Genradix would have the same issues, so then you can cross two off your list

I think this [1] is the relevant piece of code for kprobes. The address is the text position where the probe was inserted and it maps it to the structure that hold the callbacks and so on.

[1] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source/kernel/kprobes.c#L376
Okay, so they use a hash table.
